---
title: "Module 01: Git Conceptual Foundation"
description: "Understand what Git does internally before you learn the commands."
module: 1
prerequisites:
  - 0
learning_objectives:
  - "Learner can explain the difference between snapshots and diffs"
  - "Learner can describe the three states: working directory, staging area, repository"
  - "Learner can list and describe the contents of the .git directory"
  - "Learner can explain what a commit hash represents"
  - "Learner can describe branches as movable pointers"
  - "Learner can identify a detached HEAD state"
platform_coverage:
  - macos
  - linux
  - windows-gitbash
  - windows-wsl2
---

## Overview

Most Git confusion comes from using commands without understanding what Git is doing.
This module builds the mental model first — so that when you run `git add` or
`git commit`, you know exactly what is happening inside your repository.

We will explore the `.git` directory directly using the terminal commands from Module 00.

## Prerequisites

- [Module 00 — Terminal Fundamentals](00-terminal-fundamentals.qmd)

You should be able to `cd`, `ls`, and `cat` files before continuing.

---

### What Is Version Control?

**Version control**: a system that records changes to files over time so that
you can recall specific versions later.

Without version control, researchers track versions manually:

```text
analysis.R
analysis_v2.R
analysis_v2_FINAL.R
analysis_v2_FINAL_corrected.R
analysis_v2_FINAL_corrected_USE_THIS_ONE.R
```

This is error-prone, takes disk space for every copy, and makes it impossible to
understand *what* changed between versions or *why*.

Git solves this by recording a complete history of your project — who changed what,
when, and with a message explaining why — in a hidden folder called `.git`.

---

### Git's Mental Model: Snapshots, Not Diffs

Most version control systems store **diffs** — the line-by-line changes between
versions. Git is different: it stores **snapshots** of your entire project at
each point in time.

```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
flowchart LR
    subgraph Diff-based
        D1[Version 1] -->|"+ line 5\n- line 12"| D2[Version 2]
        D2 -->|"+ lines 3-4"| D3[Version 3]
    end
    subgraph Snapshot-based Git
        S1[Snapshot 1\nall files] --> S2[Snapshot 2\nall files]
        S2 --> S3[Snapshot 3\nall files]
    end
```

When a file has not changed between snapshots, Git stores only a pointer to the
previous version of that file — it does not duplicate it. This makes Git both
efficient and conceptually clean: every commit is a complete picture of your project.

---

### The Three States

Every file in a Git repository is in one of three states:

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    W["Working Directory<br/>(your files on disk)"]
    S["Staging Area<br/>(the index)"]
    R["Repository<br/>(.git/objects)"]

    W -->|"git add"| S
    S -->|"git commit"| R
    R -->|"git checkout / git restore"| W
```

- **Working directory**: your files as they exist on disk — what you see in
  Finder/File Explorer
- **Staging area** (also called the *index*): a preparation area where you assemble
  the next snapshot. You choose exactly which changes to include.
- **Repository**: the `.git` directory — the permanent record of every snapshot
  you have committed

Understanding these three states explains why `git add` exists. You do not have to
commit everything you changed — you can carefully select what goes into each snapshot.

---

### Exploring the .git Directory

The `.git` directory is where Git stores everything. Let us create a repository and
look inside.

```bash
cd ~/rehab-research
git init
```

```text
Initialized empty Git repository in /Users/yourname/rehab-research/.git/
```

Now list the `.git` directory:

```bash
ls -la .git/
```

```text
total 24
drwxr-xr-x   9 yourname  staff  288 Feb 28 09:15 .
drwxr-xr-x   5 yourname  staff  160 Feb 28 09:15 ..
-rw-r--r--   1 yourname  staff   23 Feb 28 09:15 HEAD        # your current position
-rw-r--r--   1 yourname  staff   92 Feb 28 09:15 config      # repository settings
-rw-r--r--   1 yourname  staff   73 Feb 28 09:15 description # ignored except by GitWeb
drwxr-xr-x   2 yourname  staff   64 Feb 28 09:15 hooks/      # automation scripts
drwxr-xr-x   3 yourname  staff   96 Feb 28 09:15 info/
drwxr-xr-x   4 yourname  staff  128 Feb 28 09:15 objects/    # Git's object database
drwxr-xr-x   4 yourname  staff  128 Feb 28 09:15 refs/       # branches and tags
```

Each entry has a purpose. The important ones:

#### `objects/` — Git's Database

```bash
ls .git/objects/
```

```text
info  pack
```

Right now it is almost empty — you have not committed anything yet. After a commit,
Git stores **blobs** (file contents), **trees** (directory listings), and **commits**
(metadata + pointer to a tree) here. Each is identified by a SHA-1 hash.

#### `refs/` — Branches and Tags

```bash
ls .git/refs/
```

```text
heads  tags
```

```bash
ls .git/refs/heads/
```

```text
(empty — no branches have a commit yet)
```

After your first commit, `refs/heads/main` will appear as a file containing
the hash of the latest commit. That is all a branch is: a file containing a hash.

#### `HEAD` — Your Current Position

```bash
cat .git/HEAD
```

```text
ref: refs/heads/main
```

**`HEAD`** is a pointer to where you currently are in the repository. Right now it
points at `refs/heads/main` (which does not exist yet because there are no commits).
After commits, it will point to the latest commit on your current branch.

#### `config` — Repository Configuration

```bash
cat .git/config
```

```text
[core]
	repositoryformatversion = 0
	filemode = true
	bare = false
	logallrefupdates = true
```

This is your repository's local configuration — it overrides your global
`~/.gitconfig` for this repository only.

#### `index` — The Staging Area

The `index` file does not exist until you stage something (`git add`). It is a
binary file that records the exact state of what you are about to commit.

---

### Commits as Snapshots with Metadata

Let us create a file and make a commit so we can inspect the resulting objects:

```bash
echo "library(dplyr)" > scripts/analysis.R
git add scripts/analysis.R
git commit -m "Add initial data cleaning script"
```

```text
[main (root-commit) 3f2a1b4] Add initial data cleaning script
 1 file changed, 1 insertion(+)
 create mode 100644 scripts/analysis.R
```

The string `3f2a1b4` is the beginning of your **commit hash** — a 40-character
SHA-1 fingerprint of the entire snapshot. Now look at the objects directory:

```bash
ls .git/objects/
```

```text
3f  a8  pack  info
```

```bash
git cat-file -t 3f2a1b4
```

```text
commit
```

```bash
git cat-file -p 3f2a1b4
```

```text
tree a8c3d5e2f1...
author Your Name <your@institution.edu> 1709114400 -0500
committer Your Name <your@institution.edu> 1709114400 -0500

Add initial data cleaning script
```

A commit object contains:

- **tree**: pointer to the snapshot (the directory listing at this point)
- **parent**: pointer to the previous commit (none for the first commit)
- **author** / **committer**: who made the change and when
- **message**: your description of the change

---

### The Commit Graph as a DAG

As you make more commits, Git builds a **directed acyclic graph** (DAG) —
a chain of commits where each one points back to its parent(s).

```{mermaid}
 gitGraph
   commit id: "3f2a1b4 Initial script"
   commit id: "9d4c7e1 Add normalization"
   commit id: "1a5f3b2 Fix baseline calc"

```

"Directed" means each commit knows its parent (arrows point backwards in time).
"Acyclic" means there are no loops — history always moves forward.

This graph is the foundation of branching, merging, and rebasing — everything
in the later modules builds on it.

---

### Branches as Movable Pointers

Now look at what the branch file contains after your commit:

```bash
cat .git/refs/heads/main
```

```text
3f2a1b4c8d9e0f1a2b3c4d5e6f7a8b9c0d1e2f3a
```

That is the full commit hash. **A branch is nothing more than a file containing
a single commit hash.** When you make a new commit, Git updates this file to
point to the new commit automatically.

```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
flowchart LR
    C1["commit 3f2a1b4\nInitial script"] --> C2["commit 9d4c7e1\nAdd normalization"]
    C2 --> C3["commit 1a5f3b2\nFix baseline calc"]
    MAIN["main"] --> C3
    HEAD["HEAD"] --> MAIN

    style MAIN fill:#50fa7b,color:#000
    style HEAD fill:#bd93f9,color:#000
```

When you create a new branch, Git creates a new file in `refs/heads/` pointing
to the same commit. Branches are cheap because they are just tiny files.

---

### HEAD and Detached HEAD

`HEAD` normally points to a **branch name** (e.g., `refs/heads/main`), which in
turn points to a commit. This is the **attached** state.

```bash
cat .git/HEAD
```

```text
ref: refs/heads/main
```

If you check out a commit directly (by hash or tag), HEAD points to the commit
itself — not to a branch. This is called **detached HEAD**:

```bash
# Hypothetical — do not run this yet
git checkout 3f2a1b4
cat .git/HEAD
```

```text
3f2a1b4c8d9e0f1a2b3c4d5e6f7a8b9c0d1e2f3a
```

In detached HEAD, any commits you make will not belong to any branch and can be
lost. Module 10 covers how to recover from this. For now, just know the concept.

---

## Hands-on Exercise

**[Exercises 01 →](../exercises/01-exercises.qmd)**

**Quick check**: After your first commit, run:

```bash
git log --oneline
```

```text
3f2a1b4 (HEAD -> main) Add initial data cleaning script
```

If you see a commit with your message, Git is tracking your work.

## What's Next

In **[Module 02 — Basic Git Workflow](02-basic-workflow.qmd)**, you will learn the
daily commands — `git status`, `git add`, `git commit`, `git log`, and `git diff` —
and write your first research-grade commit history.
